standard.title: Pure Substances and Mixtures
standard.description: Use a particle model to describe differences between pure substances and mixtures and apply understanding of properties of substances to separate mixtures
standard.prior_knowledge: Students can classify materials as solids, liquids or gases and have an introductory particle model from AC9S7U05. They have not formally distinguished pure substances from mixtures at the particle level or applied separation techniques systematically.
standard.progression_endpoint: Students use a particle model to explain the difference between pure substances and mixtures, and select and apply appropriate separation techniques based on properties of substances.

nodes.label: Pure substances vs mixtures at the particle level
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can distinguish pure substances from mixtures using particle diagrams and classify familiar materials correctly
nodes.minimum_width: Distinguish pure substance from mixture using particle diagrams; classify 5 familiar materials correctly with justification
nodes.typical_width: Draw particle diagrams for a pure substance, a homogeneous mixture and a heterogeneous mixture; explain the key differences
wider: Use representations of particles to show the difference between samples of pure substances and mixtures; identify examples of each in everyday contexts
Examine different solutions and identify the solvent and solute; represent at the particle level
Investigate a borderline case (e.g. alloy, saltwater at different concentrations) — is it pure or a mixture? What does this reveal about the definition?
nodes.hinge_reason: The pure/mixture distinction at the particle level is prerequisite for all separation technique selection. Students who cannot make this distinction select inappropriate techniques and cannot explain their choices — the primary assessment task.
success_criteria: Correctly distinguishes pure substance from mixture using particle diagrams
Classifies 5 familiar materials correctly with justification
Identifies whether a mixture is homogeneous or heterogeneous

nodes.label: Properties as the basis for separation
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can explain that separation techniques exploit differences in physical properties and match property differences to techniques
nodes.minimum_width: State that separation techniques exploit differences in physical properties; match at least three property differences to a separation technique
nodes.typical_width: Analyse a mixture — identify relevant property differences and justify which technique is most appropriate, including why others would not work
wider: Analyse how physical properties of substances in mixtures (particle size, density, volatility) determine the separation technique used
Explore and compare separation methods used in a variety of situations such as in the home, recycling industries and water purification
Evaluate a separation scenario with multiple possible techniques — rank by efficiency, cost and practicality
nodes.hinge_reason: Understanding that techniques exploit property differences (not just 'removing things') is the conceptual key. Without this, students apply techniques by rote and cannot explain or adapt when scenarios change. Directly assessed.
success_criteria: States that separation exploits differences in physical properties
Correctly matches at least 3 property differences to separation techniques
Explains why a chosen technique would work for a given mixture

nodes.label: Filtration and evaporation
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can perform or describe filtration and evaporation and justify their use for appropriate mixtures
nodes.minimum_width: Correctly perform or describe filtration and evaporation; identify a mixture each technique is appropriate for and explain why using property differences
nodes.typical_width: Design a procedure to separate a given mixture using filtration and/or evaporation; predict and explain the result at the particle level
wider: Investigate and use filtration and evaporation in a range of practical contexts; evaluate effectiveness
Investigate separation techniques used by First Nations Australians such as hand-picking, sieving, winnowing and filtering — explain the property differences each exploits
Investigate the effect of a variable (e.g. filter paper grade, temperature) on separation efficiency; analyse results using particle theory
success_criteria: Correctly describes or performs filtration and evaporation
Identifies a mixture appropriate for each technique with property reasoning
Predicts the result of a separation procedure at the particle level

nodes.label: Distillation and chromatography
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can describe the principles of distillation and chromatography and identify mixtures each technique is appropriate for
nodes.minimum_width: Describe the principle of distillation and chromatography; identify a mixture each is appropriate for and explain why
nodes.typical_width: Compare distillation and evaporation — explain when each is appropriate and why, using particle theory and property differences
wider: Investigate crystallisation, chromatography and distillation; explain the property differences each exploits
Investigate separation techniques used by First Nations Australians such as yandying, cold-pressing and steam distilling — connect to property principles
Analyse a chromatography result — calculate Rf values, identify components, evaluate what the result reveals about mixture composition
success_criteria: Describes the principle of distillation and chromatography
Identifies a mixture appropriate for each technique
Distinguishes when distillation is preferred over evaporation

nodes.label: Selecting and justifying separation techniques
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can select and justify an appropriate separation technique for an unfamiliar mixture using property differences
nodes.minimum_width: Given an unfamiliar mixture, select an appropriate technique and justify the choice using property differences
nodes.typical_width: Design a multi-step separation procedure for a mixture with three components; justify each step and predict the outcome
wider: Evaluate a real-world separation process (e.g. water purification, mining, food processing) — identify techniques used and property differences exploited
Compare industrial and First Nations separation techniques for the same type of mixture — analyse efficiency, sustainability and cultural significance
Design and evaluate an original separation procedure for a novel mixture; assess against criteria of efficiency, cost and practicality
success_criteria: Selects an appropriate technique for an unfamiliar mixture
Justifies the choice using property differences
Identifies at least one technique that would not work and explains why
